notes.anchor: I scarcely entrust them even to my own copyists
notes.note: Sensitive letters were dictated to or copied by trusted librarii (slave secretaries); Cicero's reluctance signals how dangerous his candour about the dynasts had become.
The bargain was for ten million sesterces (HS quadragena, 40,000 per century or tribe); the bribe Memmius and Domitius promised the sitting consuls in return for help at the polls.

The lex curiata de imperio, a formal grant of authority voted by the comitia curiata, was needed to validate a magistrate's imperium; the conspirators planned to fake augural testimony that it had been passed.

notes.anchor: the decree on the consular provinces
notes.note: Under the lex Sempronia the Senate assigned the provinces the consuls would govern after office; the plotters planned to have consulars falsely swear they had attended a meeting that never took place.
Tacitum iudicium: a proposed secret, non-public vote by jury-panels passing judgement on each candidate's conduct before the elections, an extraordinary anti-bribery measure.
The tribunes of the plebs could intercede (intercessio) on behalf of citizens; the jurors invoked this protection to refuse judging without an order of the people.
A proverbial byword for a town of fools (Greek Abdera in Thrace); Cicero exclaims it to mark the farcical muddle of the Senate's self-contradicting decrees.
Obnuntiatio: the announcement of an unfavourable omen by a magistrate or augur, which could halt or postpone public business such as elections; here used obstructively day after day.

notes.anchor: the day before the Kalends of October
notes.note: 30 September by the inclusive Roman count; the Kalends fell on the first of the month.
Tributim: canvassing voters organized by their voting tribes, the units in which the assembly cast its votes; a euphemism here for systematic bribery.
Nundinae, the eight-day market cycle, served as a rough calendar unit; Cicero doubts the electoral crisis will be resolved within a few such cycles.
M. Porcius Cato (Uticensis), the Stoic and uncompromising defender of the old order; his illness means the one truly free voice will be absent from the Senate.
Caesar's second expedition to Britain in 54 BC; Cicero's brother Quintus was serving on Caesar's staff and sent eyewitness reports.

notes.anchor: not a scruple of silver on that island
notes.note: A scrupulum was the smallest Roman weight (about 1.14 grams); Cicero jokes that Britain held no treasure worth the campaign, only unschooled slaves.
L. Aemilius Paulus, restoring the Basilica Aemilia in the Forum; Caesar secretly financed the work to win his support, part of the lavish building boom Cicero describes.
Sescenties HS (60,000,000 sesterces): the enormous sum Caesar's agents, including Cicero, paid private owners to clear land for the new Forum and voting precincts.
The Atrium Libertatis on the edge of the Forum housed the censors' records and freed-slave registers; the planned forum extension would reach as far as it.

notes.anchor: the precincts for the tribal elections
notes.note: The Saepta on the Campus Martius, the enclosures where the tribal assembly voted; Caesar's plan rebuilt them grandly in marble with a surrounding portico.
The lustrum was the purificatory ceremony closing the census; with the censors of 54 paralysed by the scandals, its completion was 'despaired of'.
An otherwise unknown law named in a corrupt passage; the manuscript reading 'Coctia' is uncertain and may conceal a different statute.
